title: Spin-wave phase modulation using magnetic domain walls in dipolarly coupled structures for non-volatile magnonic computation
authors: Hanadi Mortada, Philipp Pirro, Alexandre Abbass Hamadeh
abstract: A controllable phase shifter is a key component for spin-wave-based logic and information processing devices. Here, we propose a domain-wall-position-controlled spin-wave phase shifter that exploits dipolar coupling between two closely spaced waveguides to enable continuous phase tuning over a range approaching 360degrees while keeping the spin-wave amplitude constant. Using micromagnetic simulations, we model a bias-free hybrid structure composed of a nanoscale waveguide magnetostatically coupled to a half-ring-shaped structure both made from bismuth-doped yttrium iron garnet with strong perpendicular magnetic anisotropy. Displacing a domain wall in the half-ring modulates the dispersion relation in the adjacent straight waveguide due to the changed magnetostatic interaction, providing a compact and dynamically reconfigurable phase-shifting mechanism. This approach offers precise and non-volatile control over spin-wave propagation and is compatible with energy-efficient magnonic logic architectures.
summary: This work proposes a novel, non-volatile phase shifter for magnonics by coupling a straight spin-wave waveguide to a half-ring containing a magnetic domain wall. By moving the DW, the local magnetic field and the spin-wave dispersion relation are dynamically altered, enabling continuous phase tuning without external bias fields or significant energy loss.
main_problem: The core challenge is developing a controllable, non-volatile, and energy-efficient phase shifter for spin-wave (SW)-based logic and information processing.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that displacing a magnetic domain wall (DW) in an adjacent waveguide can continuously tune the SW phase shift over a wide range by modulating the local dispersion relation via dipolar coupling.
method: The study relies on micromagnetic simulations to model the coupled magnetostrictive system and analyze the resulting spin-wave propagation characteristics.
model_or_system: The physical system is a hybrid structure: a straight nanoscale waveguide dipolarly coupled to a half-ring waveguide, both made of Bi-doped Yttrium Iron Garnet (Bi:YIG). The phase shift is controlled by the static position of a magnetic domain wall (DW) within the half-ring.
key_observables: The primary observable is the tunable phase shift ($\Delta\varphi$) of the spin waves, which is shown to be strongly dependent on the DW position ($x_{	ext{DW}}$).
important_parameters: Key parameters include the coupling gap ($\delta = 10 	ext{ nm}$), the material properties of Bi:YIG, and the operating frequency (e.g., 5.9 GHz).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the DW maintains a stable Bloch-type configuration and that the phase tuning mechanism is dominated by dispersion engineering rather than scattering losses.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the geometry, the spatial distribution of the effective magnetic field ($	ext{B}_{	ext{eff}}$) modulated by the DW position, and the resulting continuous, non-linear dependence of the phase shift ($\Delta\varphi$) on the DW position.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the need for a controllable phase shifter, details the hybrid dipolar-coupled structure, uses micromagnetic simulations to model the field and phase shift dependence on DW position, and concludes by emphasizing the non-volatile and energy-efficient nature of the mechanism.
why_interesting: While the core physics is classical magnetism, the concept of using localized, non-dissipative field perturbations (like those from DWs) to engineer wave properties (like phase) is analogous to controlling boundary conditions or potentials in quantum wave mechanics, making it relevant to understanding engineered quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Schematic top view of the proposed Bi:YIG phase shifter consisting of a straight nanowaveg- uide dipolarly coupled to a half-ring waveguide separated by a 10 nm gap. SWs are excited at the left input port by a microwave an- tenna (yellow) at frequency f = 5.9 GHz with a fixed initial phase φin. The half-ring hosts a magnetic DW whose position xDW can be displaced along the ring. The color map shows the perpendicular magnetization component Mz in the half-ring, illustrating the 180◦

Figure 1 (shown in yellow). SW propagation is initiated by ap- plying a sinusoidal magnetic field in time, by = b0 sin(2π ft), with an amplitude of b0 = 1mT and a selected microwave fre- quency f of 5.9 GHz. The antenna excites forward-volume SWs (FVSW), which subsequently traverse the coupling re- gion and emerge from the output port with a modified phase. In the proposed design, the DW in the half-ring creates a localized perturbation of the magnetic environment, which is transferred non-locally to the straight waveguide by dipolar coupling, as illustrated in Figure 2. FIG. 3. Quantitative extraction of the domain-wall-controlled spin-wave phase shift. Extracted phase difference ∆φ = φin −φout as a function of DW position xDW, obtained from the phase of the FFT component at f = 5.9 GHz. The inset shows a demonstration of the effective field variation on the dispersion relation in the coupling region shown for three representative DW positions: x1 = +180 nm (green), x2 = 0 nm (blue), and x3 = −180 nm (red).
